content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mel Price 
Deputy County Counsel 
County of Sacramento 
700 H Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Price: 
Tedtftical Anllta_ 
(916) 322-5662 
Ad .. I,dltnltlon •• 
Executl ... /LteaI •• 
Enf ___ 
322..s660 
322~90J 
322 .... ' 
September 28, 1984 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice: 
Our Advice No. A-84-245 
This letter is to confirm telephone advice rendered to you 
at your request on September 11, 1984. 
Your request related to 
the valuation (and possible disclosure) of a gift being tendered 
to certain County employees who had been involved in obtaining 
underwriting for a bond issue. 
The Bank of America ("BofA") was the lender who underwrote 
the bond issue. 
To show its appreciation, BofA wants to give a 
wall clock to each of the four individual County officials 
involved. 
Each clock would bear a personalized inscription to 
the specific official who is its recipient. 
It is estimated 
that each clock would cost BofA several hundred dollars. 
The 
officials have sought your advice regarding the valuation of 
such a gift if received. 
You have been informed that, in a similar circumstance in 
Los Angeles County, the officials were advised that because of 
the personalized inscription the clock has no intrinsic value 
and, therfore, was not reportable. 
You requested our 
interpretation. 
I advised you that such a valuation would not be 
appropriate.!/ 
I cited to you the Cory Opinion, 1 FPPC 
!/ For instance, a new Cadillac which has a personalized, 
engraved name plate does not lose its "intrinsic value" and 
become worthless because of the name plate. 
In addition, some 
items, such as wall plaques, derive most of their value from 
being personalized. 
Again, this does not make .them valueless. 


Mel Price 
September 28, 1984 
Page 2 
Opinions 153 at 154, which requires a good faith estimate and 
suggests that the cost to the donor be utilized where such 
information is available. 
In this case, you apparently have 
estimates that the cost bo BofA will be "several hundred 
dollars." 
Obviously, then, the value would exceed the $50 gift 
reporting threshold. 
In addition, if the value equals $250 or 
more, disqualification may be required for the following 
12-month period as to decisions involving BofA. 
-
Of course, the officials can return the gifts, donate them 
to charity (without claiming a tax deduction) or pay for them 
within 30 days of receipt and thereby eliminate any reporting or 
disqualification obligations. 
Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to 
contact me at 916/322-5901. 
REL:km 
cc: 
Mr. Ed Pozorski 
Deputy County Counsel 
Los Angeles County

sections.question: The County officials sought advice regarding the valuation of personalized wall clocks being offered as gifts by Bank of America for their involvement in obtaining underwriting for a bond issue, and whether the personalized inscription made them non-reportable as having no intrinsic value.
sections.conclusion: Such a valuation would not be appropriate. The cost to the donor should be utilized where available. In this case, the clocks are estimated to cost 'several hundred dollars,' which would exceed the $50 gift reporting threshold. If the value equals $250 or more, disqualification may be required for the following 12-month period as to decisions involving Bank of America. The officials may return the gifts, donate them to charity (without claiming a tax deduction), or pay for them within 30 days of receipt to eliminate reporting or disqualification obligations.
sections.question_synthetic: Should personalized gifts (wall clocks with custom inscriptions) from Bank of America to County officials who facilitated bond underwriting be valued for disclosure purposes, and does the personalization eliminate their reportable value?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Personalization does not eliminate a gift's intrinsic value; the donor's cost estimate (several hundred dollars) should be used for valuation, exceeding the $50 reporting threshold and potentially triggering 12-month disqualification if the value reaches $250 or more. Officials may avoid these consequences by returning, donating to charity, or paying for the gifts within 30 days.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Formal advice letter confirming prior telephone consultation. Contains clear Q&A structure though not explicitly labeled as such. Footnote provides additional interpretive guidance. Strong clarity on financial thresholds and disqualification consequences.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The County officials sought advice regarding the valuation of personalized wall clocks being offered as gifts by Bank of America for their involvement in obtaining underwriting for a bond issue, and whether the personalized inscription made them non-reportable as having no intrinsic value.

CONCLUSION: Such a valuation would not be appropriate. The cost to the donor should be utilized where available. In this case, the clocks are estimated to cost 'several hundred dollars,' which would exceed the $50 gift reporting threshold. If the value equals $250 or more, disqualification may be required for the following 12-month period as to decisions involving Bank of America. The officials may return the gifts, donate them to charity (without claiming a tax deduction), or pay for them within 30 days of receipt to eliminate reporting or disqualification obligations.

' September 28, 1984 Re: Your Request for Advice: Our Advice No. A-84-245 This letter is to confirm telephone advice rendered to you at your request on September 11, 1984. Your request related to the valuation (and possible disclosure) of a gift being tendered to certain County employees who had been involved in obtaining underwriting for a bond issue. Each clock would bear a personalized inscription to the specific official who is its recipient. It is estimated that each clock would cost BofA several hundred dollars. The officials have sought your advice regarding the valuation of such a gift if received. You have been informed that, in a similar circumstance in Los Angeles County, the officials were advised that because of the personalized inscription the clock has no intrinsic value and, therfore, was not reportable. You requested our interpretation. I advised you that such a valuation would not be appropriate.!/ I cited to you the Cory Opinion, 1 FPPC !/ For instance, a new Cadillac which has a personalized, engraved name plate does not lose its "intrinsic value" and become worthless because of the name plate. In addition, some items, such as wall plaques, derive most of their value from being personalized. Again, this does not make .them valueless. Mel Price September 28, 1984 Page 2 Opinions 153 at 154, which requires a good faith estimate and suggests that the cost to the donor be utilized where such information is available. In this case, you apparently have estimates that the cost bo BofA will be "several hundred dollars." Obviously, then, the value would exceed the $50 gift reporting threshold. embedding.summary: FPPC confirmed telephone advice to Sacramento County officials regarding the disclosure and valuation of personalized wall clock gifts from Bank of America. The FPPC rejected the argument that personalization eliminates reportable value and advised the gifts must be valued at the donor's estimated cost (several hundred dollars), triggering reporting requirements and potential disqualification.
